map.condition: Need to check if a schedule is conflict serializable
map.use: \text{Build precedence graph; conflict serializable} \iff \text{graph is acyclic}
\text{Conflict: same data item, different transactions, at least one write}
T_j \text{ reads from } T_i \Rightarrow T_i \text{ must commit before } T_j \text{ commits}

map.condition: Checking if schedule is view serializable

map.use: \text{2PL: grow phase then shrink phase} \Rightarrow \text{conflict serializable}

formulas.name: Atomicity
formulas.latex: \text{Atomicity: transaction executes completely or not at all (all-or-nothing)}
formulas.plain: Atomicity: transaction executes completely or not at all (all-or-nothing)
formulas.whenToUse: When a transaction fails midway — all changes must be rolled back
formulas.trap: Partial commits are NOT allowed; even a single operation failure requires full rollback

formulas.name: Consistency
formulas.latex: \text{Consistency: DB moves from one valid state to another valid state}
formulas.plain: Consistency: DB moves from one valid state to another valid state
formulas.whenToUse: Integrity constraints must hold before and after a transaction
formulas.trap: Consistency is a property the application (not the DBMS) must enforce in most cases

formulas.name: Isolation
formulas.latex: \text{Isolation: concurrent transactions appear as if executed serially}
formulas.plain: Isolation: concurrent transactions appear as if executed serially
formulas.whenToUse: Ensuring intermediate states of a transaction are invisible to others

terms.means: transactions execute one after another with no interleaving

formulas.trap: Isolation does NOT mean transactions actually run serially; it means the result is equivalent

formulas.name: Durability
formulas.latex: \text{Durability: committed transactions persist despite system failures}
formulas.plain: Durability: committed transactions persist despite system failures
formulas.whenToUse: After a transaction commits, its effects are permanent even after crash
formulas.trap: Durability is maintained by logs/write-ahead logging (WAL), not just memory

formulas.name: Conflicting Operations
formulas.latex: O_i, O_j \text{ conflict} \iff \text{same data item} \wedge \text{different transactions} \wedge \text{at least one is a write}
formulas.plain: Operations Oi, Oj conflict iff: same data item AND different transactions AND at least one is a write
formulas.whenToUse: Determining if swapping two adjacent operations changes the schedule outcome
formulas.trap: Two reads on the same item do NOT conflict; only W-W, R-W, or W-R pairs conflict

formulas.name: Precedence (Serialization) Graph
formulas.latex: T_i \rightarrow T_j \text{ in PG} \iff \exists \text{ conflicting ops where } T_i\text{'s op precedes } T_j\text{'s op}
formulas.plain: Ti -> Tj in precedence graph iff Ti's conflicting op precedes Tj's conflicting op
formulas.whenToUse: Testing conflict serializability by building the directed precedence graph

terms.means: T_i must precede T_j in any serial order

formulas.trap: Add an edge only for CONFLICTING pairs; non-conflicting operations do not create edges

formulas.name: Conflict Serializability Test
formulas.latex: \text{Schedule } S \text{ is conflict serializable} \iff \text{its precedence graph is acyclic (DAG)}
formulas.plain: Schedule S is conflict serializable iff its precedence graph is a DAG (no cycles)
formulas.whenToUse: Final verdict after building the precedence graph
formulas.trap: A cycle of even length is still a cycle — the schedule is NOT conflict serializable

formulas.name: Equivalent Serial Order
formulas.latex: \text{Topological sort of acyclic PG gives the equivalent serial schedule}
formulas.plain: Topological sort of acyclic precedence graph gives equivalent serial schedule
formulas.whenToUse: Identifying which serial schedules a conflict-serializable schedule is equivalent to

terms.means: linear ordering consistent with all directed edges

formulas.trap: Multiple valid topological orders may exist — each is a valid equivalent serial schedule

formulas.name: View Equivalence Conditions
formulas.latex: S \equiv_v S' \iff \begin{cases} \text{(1) Same initial reads} \\ \text{(2) Same reads-from relation} \\ \text{(3) Same final writes} \end{cases}
formulas.plain: S view-equivalent to S' iff: (1) same initial reads, (2) same reads-from pairs, (3) same final writes
formulas.whenToUse: Checking if a non-conflict-serializable schedule is still view serializable

terms.means: T_j reads value written by T_i
last write to an item in the schedule

formulas.trap: View serializability is NP-complete to check; conflict serializability is polynomial

formulas.name: Serializability Containment
formulas.latex: \text{Conflict serializable} \subsetneq \text{View serializable} \subsetneq \text{All schedules}
formulas.plain: Conflict serializable (subset) View serializable (subset) All schedules
formulas.whenToUse: Comparing the two notions of serializability

terms.means: proper subset — view serializable is strictly larger

formulas.trap: Every conflict-serializable schedule is view serializable, but not vice versa

formulas.name: Recoverable Schedule
formulas.latex: T_j \text{ reads from } T_i \Rightarrow T_i \text{ must commit before } T_j \text{ commits}
formulas.plain: If Tj reads from Ti, then Ti must commit before Tj commits
formulas.whenToUse: Checking whether a schedule allows correct rollback after failure

terms.means: T_j reads a value last written by T_i

formulas.trap: If Tj commits before Ti commits and Ti later aborts, the committed data of Tj is wrong — irrecoverable

formulas.name: Cascadeless (ACA) Schedule
formulas.latex: T_j \text{ reads } X \text{ from } T_i \Rightarrow T_i \text{ must commit before } T_j \text{ reads } X
formulas.plain: If Tj reads X written by Ti, then Ti must have committed before Tj reads X
formulas.whenToUse: Avoiding cascading aborts — once Ti aborts, Tj need not abort too
formulas.trap: Cascadeless is stricter than recoverable; cascadeless implies recoverable but not vice versa

formulas.name: Strict Schedule
formulas.latex: T_j \text{ reads/writes } X \text{ written by } T_i \Rightarrow T_i \text{ must commit or abort before } T_j \text{ reads/writes } X
formulas.plain: Tj can read or write X only after Ti (which last wrote X) has committed or aborted
formulas.whenToUse: Simplifying recovery — no need to look at transaction history for undo
formulas.trap: Strict implies cascadeless implies recoverable — all three are nested classes

formulas.name: Recoverability Hierarchy
formulas.latex: \text{Strict} \subsetneq \text{Cascadeless (ACA)} \subsetneq \text{Recoverable} \subsetneq \text{All schedules}
formulas.plain: Strict (subset) Cascadeless (subset) Recoverable (subset) All schedules
formulas.whenToUse: Classifying a given schedule into its recoverability category
formulas.trap: An irrecoverable schedule is NOT in any of the three safe classes — DBMS must prevent these

formulas.name: Two-Phase Locking (2PL)
formulas.latex: \text{2PL: Growing phase (acquire locks)} \rightarrow \text{Lock point} \rightarrow \text{Shrinking phase (release locks)}
formulas.plain: 2PL: Growing phase (only acquire) -> Lock point -> Shrinking phase (only release)
formulas.whenToUse: Guaranteeing conflict serializability in concurrent execution

terms.means: moment when last lock is acquired

formulas.trap: 2PL prevents non-serializable schedules but does NOT prevent deadlock

formulas.name: Strict 2PL
formulas.latex: \text{Strict 2PL: all exclusive (write) locks held until } \textit{commit/abort}
formulas.plain: Strict 2PL: hold all exclusive locks until transaction commits or aborts
formulas.whenToUse: When both serializability AND strict recoverability are needed

terms.means: write lock; no other transaction may read or write

formulas.trap: Strict 2PL produces strict schedules; basic 2PL can still produce cascading aborts

formulas.name: Lock Compatibility Matrix
formulas.latex: \begin{array}{c|cc} & S & X \\ \hline S & \checkmark & \times \\ X & \times & \times \end{array}
formulas.plain: Shared(S) and Shared(S): compatible. Shared+Exclusive or Exclusive+Exclusive: incompatible.
formulas.whenToUse: Deciding whether a lock request can be granted immediately
formulas.trap: Two reads (S+S) are compatible; mixing any exclusive lock causes a wait

formulas.name: Deadlock Conditions
formulas.latex: \text{Deadlock: mutual wait cycle } T_1 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow T_1
formulas.plain: Deadlock: circular wait — T1 waits for T2, T2 waits for ... , Tn waits for T1
formulas.whenToUse: Identifying or preventing deadlock in concurrent locking scenarios
formulas.trap: Deadlock detection uses a wait-for graph; a cycle in the wait-for graph signals deadlock

formulas.name: Timestamp Ordering (TO) Protocol
formulas.latex: \text{If } TS(T_i) < TS(T_j): \; T_i\text{'s ops must precede }T_j\text{'s conflicting ops}
formulas.plain: Timestamp ordering: older transaction (smaller TS) must logically precede newer one
formulas.whenToUse: Lock-free concurrency control using transaction timestamps

terms.means: timestamp assigned to transaction T at start

formulas.trap: A transaction is rolled back and restarted (with a NEW timestamp) when the rule is violated — not simply delayed

formulas.name: Wound-Wait vs Wait-Die (Deadlock Prevention)
formulas.latex: \text{Wait-Die: older waits, younger dies (aborts)}\quad\text{Wound-Wait: older wounds (aborts) younger, younger waits}
formulas.plain: Wait-Die: older transaction waits, younger is aborted. Wound-Wait: older wounds (aborts) younger, younger waits for older.
formulas.whenToUse: Preventing deadlock by aborting one transaction when a conflict arises
formulas.trap: Both schemes always abort the YOUNGER transaction; they differ in which role (requester or holder) it plays

formulas.name: Dirty Read (WR Conflict Anomaly)
formulas.latex: T_1 \xrightarrow{W(X)} T_2 \xrightarrow{R(X)} \text{ then } T_1 \text{ aborts} \Rightarrow \text{dirty read}
formulas.plain: T1 writes X, T2 reads X, then T1 aborts -> T2 read a dirty (uncommitted) value
formulas.whenToUse: Identifying irrecoverable or non-cascadeless schedule scenarios
formulas.trap: Dirty read creates an irrecoverable schedule if T2 commits before T1's abort is known

formulas.name: Lost Update (WW Conflict Anomaly)
formulas.latex: T_1: W(X) \;\|\; T_2: W(X) \Rightarrow \text{one write is overwritten (lost)}
formulas.plain: T1 and T2 both write X; one write is lost because the other overwrites it
formulas.whenToUse: Detecting the lost-update problem in concurrent write scenarios
formulas.trap: Lost update is a W-W conflict; prevented by exclusive locking before any write

formulas.name: Unrepeatable Read (RW Conflict Anomaly)
formulas.latex: T_1: R(X), \; T_2: W(X),\text{commit}, \; T_1: R(X) \Rightarrow \text{different values}
formulas.plain: T1 reads X, T2 writes and commits X, T1 reads X again and sees a different value
formulas.whenToUse: Detecting inconsistent reads within the same transaction
formulas.trap: Prevented by holding shared locks on X until T1 commits (2PL rule for reads)
